mnemonics.title: Derangement Values
mnemonics.content: Remember D(0)=1, D(1)=0. Then D(2)=1, D(3)=2, D(4)=9, D(5)=44, D(6)=265. Pattern: Multiply previous by position, then add/subtract previous-previous. 4×2+1=9, 9×3+2=44, 44×4+9=265. That's the derangement key!

mnemonics.title: Recurrence Memory
mnemonics.content: For D(n), think 'n-1 friends'. First friend has n-1 choices. Either they swap with original owner (leaving n-2 to derange) or they don't (leaving n-1 to derange). So (n-1)[D(n-1) + D(n-2)].

acronyms.usage: Number of permutations with no fixed points
Limit of n!/D(n) as n→∞, D(n) ≈ n!/e

visualTips.visualization: Imagine placing element 1. It goes to position k (k≠1). Now element k has two choices: go to position 1 (forming 2-cycle with element 1) or go somewhere else. These two cases lead to D(n-2) and D(n-1) respectively.
Think of the Taylor series expansion: e^x = 1 + x + x²/2! + x³/3! + ... At x = -1, we get e^-1 = 1 - 1 + 1/2! - 1/3! + ... = 1/e. This series appears in derangement formula.

analogies.analogy: Like n people at a party dropping their hats, then randomly picking them up. Derangements count the ways where everyone gets the wrong hat.
Like n couples at a round table where husbands and wives cannot sit next to each other - everyone must be displaced from their natural partner.
Think of a classroom where students must all move to different seats - no one stays in their original seat. That's a derangement!
